findings.title: Potential G704: SSRF via taint analysis
findings.description:
SSRF via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the targetURL parameter (e.g., via query string or form input).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request to the vulnerable endpoint with targetURL set to an internal IP (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). 2. The handler at main.go:157 calls http.Get(targetURL) without validation. 3. The server makes an HTTP request to the attacker-specified URL, potentially accessing internal services.
attack_scenario.impact: The attacker can probe internal network services, access cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., AWS IAM credentials), or perform port scanning of internal hosts, leading to potential data exfiltration or lateral movement.
attack_scenario.explanation: The code directly uses user-controlled input in an HTTP request without any allowlist or validation, making it a classic SSRF vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G701: SQL injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
SQL injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
71 | 70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)
72:

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can supply username and password parameters (e.g., via login form).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a POST request with username=' OR '1'='1' -- and password=anything. 2. The code at main.go:70 concatenates these directly into the SQL query. 3. The query becomes: SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' --' AND password='anything'. 4. The database returns all users, bypassing authentication.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can bypass authentication and gain unauthorized access to any user account, potentially escalating privileges or accessing sensitive data.
attack_scenario.explanation: String concatenation of user input into SQL queries is a textbook SQL injection vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G703: Path traversal via taint analysis
findings.description:
Path traversal via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the filename parameter (e.g., via query string).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with filename=../../etc/passwd. 2. The code at main.go:135 constructs filepath as ./files/../../etc/passwd, which resolves to /etc/passwd. 3. os.ReadFile at line 136 reads the file and returns its contents.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary files on the server filesystem, including configuration files, source code, or sensitive data (e.g., /etc/shadow, database credentials).
attack_scenario.explanation: The code prepends a directory but does not sanitize path traversal sequences, allowing access outside the intended directory.

findings.title: Potential G702: Command injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
Command injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[Also reported by another step: Subprocess launched with variable
Gosec confidence: 90%]

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the cmd parameter (e.g., via query string or form input).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with cmd=ls; cat /etc/passwd. 2. The code at main.go:114 passes cmd directly to exec.Command("bash", "-c", cmd). 3. The shell executes the attacker's command, including the injected cat /etc/passwd.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary system commands on the server, leading to full server compromise, data exfiltration, or lateral movement.
attack_scenario.explanation: Passing user input to bash -c without sanitization allows arbitrary command injection.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the input parameter (e.g., via query string).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with input=<script>alert('XSS')</script>. 2. The code at main.go:210 writes the input directly into a JSON response without escaping. 3. If the response is rendered in a browser (e.g., via a JSONP callback or if Content-Type is overridden), the script executes.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
attack_scenario.explanation: Even with Content-Type: application/json, if the response is consumed by a script that evaluates it (e.g., JSONP), XSS is possible. Additionally, older browsers may ignore the content type.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The response has Content-Type: application/octet-stream, which forces a download. No browser will render the content as HTML, so XSS is not possible.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The response has Content-Type: application/octet-stream, which forces a download. No browser will render the content as HTML, so XSS is not possible.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The response has Content-Type: text/plain, which prevents HTML rendering. Browsers will display the content as plain text, not execute scripts.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The response has Content-Type: text/plain, which prevents HTML rendering. Browsers will display the content as plain text, not execute scripts.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the html parameter (e.g., via query string or form input).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with html=<script>alert('XSS')</script>. 2. The code at main.go:100 writes the html directly to the response with no Content-Type header (defaults to text/html). 3. The browser renders the script and executes it.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or phishing.
attack_scenario.explanation: Writing user-controlled HTML directly to the response without escaping or setting a safe Content-Type is a classic reflected XSS vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G401: Use of weak cryptographic primitive
findings.description:
Use of weak cryptographic primitive
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
206 | 205: 	// VULNERABLE: MD5 is cryptographically broken
206: 	hash := md5.Sum([]byte(input))
207: 	hexHash := fmt.Sprintf("%x", hash)

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can obtain MD5 hashes (e.g., via the hash endpoint or database leaks).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains MD5 hashes from the application (e.g., via the hash endpoint at main.go:206). 2. Attacker uses precomputed rainbow tables or brute-force to reverse the MD5 hash. 3. Attacker recovers the original input (e.g., password).
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can recover plaintext values from MD5 hashes, compromising user passwords or other sensitive data.
attack_scenario.explanation: MD5 is cryptographically broken and vulnerable to collision and preimage attacks. Its use for hashing sensitive data is insecure.

findings.title: Potential G114: Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
findings.description:
Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker with network access to the server (e.g., on the same LAN or via a compromised host).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a slow HTTP request (e.g., slowloris) to the server. 2. Because no timeouts are configured, the server keeps the connection open indefinitely. 3. The attacker exhausts server resources (connections, memory), causing denial of service.
attack_scenario.impact: Denial of service: the server becomes unresponsive to legitimate requests, impacting availability.
attack_scenario.explanation: Using http.ListenAndServe without timeouts makes the application vulnerable to slow HTTP attacks and resource exhaustion.

attack_scenario.actor: Same as IRON-GOSEC-002: unauthenticated attacker controlling username and password.
attack_scenario.path: Same as IRON-GOSEC-002: SQL injection via string concatenation.
attack_scenario.impact: Same as IRON-GOSEC-002: authentication bypass, unauthorized data access.

findings.description:
Potential file inclusion via variable

attack_scenario.path: Same as IRON-GOSEC-003: path traversal via filename parameter.

findings.description:
Potential HTTP request made with variable url

attack_scenario.actor: Same as IRON-GOSEC-001: attacker controlling targetURL.
attack_scenario.path: Same as IRON-GOSEC-001: SSRF via http.Get.
attack_scenario.impact: Same as IRON-GOSEC-001: internal network access, cloud metadata exposure.

findings.title: Potential G501: Blocklisted import crypto/md5: weak cryptographic primitive
findings.description:
Blocklisted import crypto/md5: weak cryptographic primitive
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
11 | 10: import (
11: 	"crypto/md5"
12: 	"database/sql"

attack_scenario.actor: Any developer or attacker who can analyze the codebase.
attack_scenario.path: 1. The code imports crypto/md5 at line 11. 2. This import is used in the hash endpoint (main.go:206). 3. The use of MD5 weakens the security of any hashed data.
attack_scenario.impact: Use of a weak cryptographic primitive (MD5) makes hashed data susceptible to collision and preimage attacks, potentially compromising data integrity and confidentiality.
attack_scenario.explanation: Importing crypto/md5 is a code quality issue that indicates the use of a deprecated and insecure algorithm.

findings.title: Potential Generic API key in config
findings.description: Regex pattern detected a potential Generic API key in config at main.go:31. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.
findings.code_snippet: 31 | const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu"

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker with access to the source code repository or compiled binary (e.g., via insider threat, leaked repo, or decompilation). No authentication required.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains the source code (e.g., from a public GitHub repo, internal leak, or by decompiling the binary). 2. Attacker reads line 31 of main.go: `const apiKey = "sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu"`. 3. Attacker uses this API key to authenticate to the external service (e.g., OpenAI, Stripe) and perform actions on behalf of the application.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker gains unauthorized access to the external service associated with the API key, potentially leading to data exfiltration, service abuse, financial charges, or privilege escalation depending on the key's permissions.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding is a hardcoded API key in source code. This is a real security vulnerability because the key is static, embedded in the code, and can be extracted by anyone with access to the codebase or binary. The key pattern 'sk-...' strongly suggests a secret key for a service like OpenAI or Stripe. The severity is high because it exposes the application to unauthorized external service access.

findings.title: SBOM generated: 1 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 1 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: Not a git repository — supply chain checks limited
findings.description: Supply chain security checks (SBOM provenance, SLSA) require a git repository. Without git history, we cannot verify commit signatures or build provenance.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).
findings.fix_suggestion: syft scan battle_test_candidates/go_target -o cyclonedx-json > sbom.cdx.json

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.
findings.fix_suggestion: go install github.com/ossf/scorecard/v5@latest

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) in proxy handler
findings.description: The handleProxy function takes a user-supplied 'url' query parameter and passes it directly to http.Get without any validation or allowlist. An attacker can make the server send requests to internal services (e.g., 169.254.169.254 for cloud metadata, localhost, internal APIs) or external systems, leading to information disclosure, port scanning, or further attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement a strict allowlist of permitted target URLs or URL patterns. Validate that the URL scheme is HTTPS and the host is in a predefined list. Alternatively, use a URL parser to check the host against an allowlist and reject any internal IP ranges.

findings.title: OS Command Injection in handleExec
findings.description: The 'cmd' query parameter is directly passed to exec.Command with bash -c, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary system commands on the server.
findings.fix_suggestion: Avoid using shell commands with user input. If necessary, validate and sanitize input strictly, or use a safer alternative like exec.Command with separate arguments.

findings.title: Path Traversal in handleFiles
findings.description: The 'filename' parameter from the URL query is concatenated into a file path without validation, allowing an attacker to read arbitrary files via path traversal sequences (e.g., '../').
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the filename to prevent path traversal: use filepath.Base() to strip directory components, or maintain an allowlist of permitted filenames.

findings.title: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) in handleSearch
findings.description: User input from the 'query' query parameter is directly concatenated into an HTML string without any escaping or sanitization, then written to the response with Content-Type text/html. An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript by crafting a malicious query parameter, leading to XSS.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use Go's html/template package to auto-escape user input, or manually escape the query using html.EscapeString() before embedding it in the HTML.

findings.title: Missing authentication on proxy endpoint
findings.description: handleProxy allows anyone to make arbitrary HTTP requests without any authentication. This can be used to access internal services, exfiltrate data, or perform SSRF attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware (e.g., session token, API key) before processing the request. Also consider restricting access to authorized users only.

findings.title: Missing input validation on URL parameter
findings.description: The 'url' parameter is taken directly from user input and used in an HTTP request without any validation or allowlist. This enables SSRF attacks, including access to internal networks and cloud metadata endpoints.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate the URL against an allowlist of permitted domains/IPs. Reject URLs that point to private/internal IP ranges (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 10.x.x.x, 172.16.x.x, 192.168.x.x, 169.254.x.x). Consider using a URL parser to validate scheme and host.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on proxy endpoint
findings.description: The proxy endpoint has no rate limiting, allowing an attacker to abuse it for large-scale SSRF scanning or data exfiltration.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP or per user to restrict the number of requests within a time window.

findings.title: Missing authentication on command execution endpoint
findings.description: handleExec executes arbitrary shell commands without any authentication check. This is a critical security flaw allowing unauthenticated remote code execution.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware (e.g., session token, API key, or JWT validation) before processing the request. Consider also restricting access to trusted IPs or using a VPN.

findings.title: Missing input validation on command parameter
findings.description: The 'cmd' parameter is taken directly from the URL query and passed to exec.Command without any validation, sanitization, or whitelisting. This allows arbitrary command injection.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement strict input validation: whitelist allowed commands/arguments, validate against a regex pattern, or use a safe API instead of shell execution. Never pass user input directly to shell commands.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on command execution endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands repeatedly, potentially causing resource exhaustion or brute-force attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting (e.g., token bucket, per-IP limits) to restrict the number of requests per time unit. Consider using middleware like 'github.com/didip/tollbooth'.

findings.title: Missing authentication on file access endpoint
findings.description: handleFiles allows reading arbitrary files without any authentication. An attacker can enumerate and read any file in the ./files/ directory.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware or check session/JWT token before processing the request.

findings.title: Missing input validation on filename parameter
findings.description: The filename parameter is used directly in file path construction without any validation. This allows path traversal attacks (e.g., '../../etc/passwd') to read arbitrary files outside the intended directory.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate filename: reject path separators, use filepath.Base() to strip directory components, and ensure the resolved path stays within the allowed directory.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on file access endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint, allowing an attacker to rapidly enumerate files or perform denial-of-service attacks by requesting many files.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting middleware (e.g., token bucket or sliding window) to restrict requests per IP or user.

findings.title: Missing authentication on search endpoint
findings.description: handleSearch returns search results without any authentication check. While not as sensitive as admin endpoints, search functionality can leak information and should be protected.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add auth middleware. Example: wrap handler with RequireAuth() or check session token at function start.

findings.title: Missing input validation on search query
findings.description: The 'query' parameter is taken directly from URL query string and reflected into HTML without any validation, sanitization, or escaping. This allows stored and reflected XSS attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate and sanitize the query parameter. Use html.EscapeString() or a proper template engine to escape output. Consider limiting query length and rejecting dangerous characters.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on search endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to the search endpoint, allowing attackers to perform automated queries, potentially leading to resource exhaustion or information gathering.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting middleware. Example: use golang.org/x/time/rate or a custom token bucket per IP.

findings.title: Missing authentication on hash endpoint
findings.description: handleHash performs cryptographic hashing without any authentication check. While not accessing user data, it exposes a hash oracle that could be used for offline attacks or information gathering.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add authentication middleware or require an API key to access this endpoint.

findings.title: Missing input validation on hash input parameter
findings.description: The 'input' query parameter is used directly without any validation. While it's hashed before output, it's reflected unsafely in the JSON response, leading to potential injection or information disclosure.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate input length and characters. Use a maximum length (e.g., 256 chars) and reject non-printable characters. Sanitize input before reflecting in JSON response.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on hash endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting is applied to this endpoint. An attacker could abuse it to perform large-scale hash computations or denial of service.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting (e.g., token bucket or sliding window) to restrict requests per IP or user.

findings.title: Missing Content-Type validation
findings.description: The handler does not validate the Content-Type header of incoming requests. It accepts any content type, which could lead to unexpected behavior or bypass of security controls.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate that the Content-Type header is 'application/json' for POST requests, or handle appropriately for GET requests.

findings.title: Missing input validation on username and password
findings.description: Username and password are taken directly from form values without any validation (type, length, format). This allows SQL injection via string concatenation.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate inputs: check for non-empty, max length, allowed characters. Use parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on login endpoint
findings.description: No rate limiting on login attempts, allowing brute-force password attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting per IP or username, e.g., using a middleware or in-memory store with exponential backoff.

findings.title: Missing CSRF protection on login endpoint
findings.description: Login is a state-changing POST operation (creates session) but no CSRF token is validated. This could allow cross-site request forgery.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add CSRF token generation and validation, e.g., using a library like gorilla/csrf.

findings.title: Missing Content-Type validation on login endpoint
findings.description: The handler uses r.FormValue which parses form data regardless of Content-Type, but does not validate that the request is a form submission. This could allow unexpected parsing behavior.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate Content-Type header is 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' or 'multipart/form-data' before processing form values.

findings.title: Missing authentication on admin user list endpoint
findings.description: handleAdminUsers returns all user data without any authentication check. Anyone can access this endpoint and enumerate all users.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add auth middleware. Example: wrap handler with RequireAuth() or check session token at function start.

findings.title: Missing input validation on admin user list endpoint
findings.description: The handler does not validate any input parameters. Although the current query is static, the lack of validation could allow injection if the endpoint is extended or if request parameters are used in the future.
findings.fix_suggestion: Validate any input parameters (e.g., query parameters) before using them in database queries. Use parameterized queries or prepared statements.

findings.title: Missing rate limiting on admin user list endpoint
findings.description: The handler does not implement rate limiting, which could allow an attacker to enumerate users or perform brute-force attacks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Implement rate limiting middleware or use a library like 'golang.org/x/time/rate' to limit requests per IP or user.
